content.text:
The Fundamental Theorem connects the two main operations of calculus: 
differentiation and integration.

content.title: First Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
content.content:
Let $f$ be integrable on $[a, b]$ and define $F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ 
for $x \in [a, b]$. Then:

1. $F$ is continuous on $[a, b]$.

2. If $f$ is continuous at $c \in (a, b)$, then $F$ is differentiable 
at $c$ and $F'(c) = f(c)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) Since $f$ is bounded, say $|f| \leq M$, we have
\[
|F(x) - F(y)| = \left|\int_y^x f\right| \leq M|x - y|.
\]
Thus $F$ is Lipschitz, hence continuous.

(b) Let $\varepsilon > 0$. Since $f$ is continuous at $c$, choose 
$\delta > 0$ so that $|t - c| < \delta$ implies $|f(t) - f(c)| < \varepsilon$.

For $0 < |h| < \delta$:
$$
\left|\frac{F(c+h) - F(c)}{h} - f(c)\right| 
&= \left|\frac{1}{h}\int_c^{c+h} f(t)\,dt - f(c)\right| \\
&= \left|\frac{1}{h}\int_c^{c+h} (f(t) - f(c))\,dt\right| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{|h|} \cdot |h| \cdot \varepsilon = \varepsilon.
$$
Thus $F'(c) = f(c)$.

content.title: Second Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
content.content:
If $F$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and differentiable on $(a, b)$ with 
$F' = f$ where $f$ is integrable on $[a, b]$, then
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $P = \{x_0, \ldots, x_n\}$ be any partition. By MVT, for each $i$, 
there exists $t_i \in (x_{i-1}, x_i)$ with 
$F(x_i) - F(x_{i-1}) = F'(t_i)(x_i - x_{i-1}) = f(t_i)(x_i - x_{i-1})$.

Summing: $F(b) - F(a) = \sum f(t_i)(x_i - x_{i-1})$.

This is a Riemann sum for $f$. As $\|P\| \to 0$, Riemann sums approach 
$\int_a^b f$, so $F(b) - F(a) = \int_a^b f$.

content.title: Integration by Substitution
content.content:
Let $g: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuously differentiable with range 
in $[c, d]$, and let $f$ be continuous on $[c, d]$. Then
\[
\int_a^b f(g(x))g'(x)\,dx = \int_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u)\,du.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Integration by Parts
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are continuously differentiable on $[a, b]$, then
\[
\int_a^b f(x)g'(x)\,dx = [f(x)g(x)]_a^b - \int_a^b f'(x)g(x)\,dx.
\]
content.label: Theorem
